questions.question: According to Dr. Naomi Eisenberger's fMRI research, which brain regions are activated by social rejection?

options.text: Only emotional processing centers unrelated to physical pain
Anterior cingulate cortex and insula—the same regions that respond to physical injury
No specific brain regions are activated by rejection

questions.explanation: Eisenberger's fMRI studies show social rejection activates the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) and insula—the exact same regions responding to physical pain like broken bones or burns. This is why rejection literally hurts: your brain evolved to make social exclusion intensely painful to motivate reconnection and group cohesion, since ancestral humans faced death without their tribe. The Tylenol effect (acetaminophen reducing both physical and social pain) validates this shared neural pathway.

questions.question: According to Dr. Kristin Neff's research, how much faster do people recover from rejection using self-compassion versus self-criticism?
questions.explanation: Self-compassion predicts 40% faster recovery from rejection compared to self-criticism. The self-criticism response ('I'm such an idiot for trying') increases suffering, shame, and avoidance, while self-compassion ('This hurts, and that's okay. Everyone experiences rejection. I can be kind to myself') reduces suffering and maintains courage to try again. The three components—self-kindness, common humanity, and mindfulness—actively speed healing rather than prolonging pain.

questions.question: What is the normal rejection rate even for socially skilled people when initiating social approaches?
questions.explanation: Even socially skilled people experience 20-40% rejection due to timing, compatibility, mood, or the other person's own anxiety—not character judgment. This normalization is crucial because people often catastrophize rejection ('I'm fundamentally unlikable') when experiencing normal statistical outcomes. Rejection reflects fit, timing, and circumstances more than personal worth. Understanding this baseline prevents overgeneralizing from individual rejections to universal unworthiness.

questions.question: What distinguishes a growth mindset from a fixed mindset response to rejection, according to Dr. Carol Dweck's research?

options.text: Growth mindset ignores rejection entirely; fixed mindset obsesses over it
Growth mindset treats rejection as data for improvement; fixed mindset treats it as evidence of unchangeable unworthiness
Both mindsets respond identically to rejection
Growth mindset means never feeling hurt by rejection

questions.explanation: Growth mindset reframes rejection as data about timing, approach, or fit ('This didn't work. I'll try again with different timing/strategy') rather than character judgment ('I'm defective and will always be rejected'). Fixed mindset leads to giving up, shame, and avoidance because rejection 'proves' permanent unworthiness. Growth mindset maintains openness to possibility while adjusting strategy, treating rejection as feedback rather than verdict. Both can feel pain—the difference is what you conclude about yourself and future possibilities.

questions.question: If you're experiencing suicidal thoughts or self-harm urges after rejection, inability to function for weeks, or chronic depression triggered by any social setback, what is the appropriate next step?

options.text: Practice more self-compassion exercises from this lesson
Accept that rejection will always devastate you and avoid all social risk
Seek professional support immediately—therapy for rejection sensitivity/attachment issues, assessment for depression/anxiety, or crisis resources (988 Lifeline)
Try rejection therapy to desensitize through more exposure

questions.explanation: These symptoms—suicidal thoughts, self-harm urges, inability to function for weeks, chronic depression from social setbacks—signal clinical-level issues requiring professional support, not self-help strategies. This may indicate severe rejection sensitivity rooted in trauma or attachment wounds, clinical depression or anxiety where rejection sensitivity is prominent, or need for DBT skills for overwhelming emotions. Crisis resources (988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline, Crisis Text Line: HOME to 741741) provide immediate support. Seeking professional care isn't weakness—it's self-compassion in action.

title: Handling Rejection & Building Resilience — The Neuroscience of Social Pain - Quiz
